close_reason: Already mitigated: put() wraps the String in Zeroizing::new() at line 150, and get() uses into_bytes() zero-copy into SecretValue. The keyring crate's internal copy is unavoidable and documented.
description:
Trigger: any get() or put() call.
Behavior: lib.rs:111 keyring::Entry::get_password() returns String. secretx-desktop calls .into_bytes() and wraps in SecretValue (which is Zeroizing). Good. But the original String, returned by keyring, lived as a cleartext heap allocation — its capacity may have been larger than its length, the bytes between len and capacity are not what we're concerned about, but more importantly the caller side: in put() at lib.rs:139-145, value.as_bytes() is decoded to UTF-8 and to_owned() into a fresh String 's' — this String holds the cleartext secret in non-zeroizing storage until it goes out of scope and the allocator overwrites it.

This is the same root cause: keyring 3.x uses &str / String for passwords, not a zeroize-aware type.

Impact: a 'secrets' crate where the secret material spends a window in non-zeroizing heap memory. In practice for desktop keychains this window is small, but it undermines the value proposition of using SecretValue.

Fix options:
1. Document the limitation prominently in lib.rs module docs and README. (cheap, honest)
2. Wrap the keyring String in an immediate zeroize step before drop — manually overwrite. Awkward because of into_bytes() consuming the String.
3. Patch keyring upstream to expose a zeroize-aware variant. Out of scope here.

Recommend: option 1 in this repo, plus a TODO referencing upstream issue.

Cross-crate: secretx-keyring/src/lib.rs has the identical pattern; file the same fix there. (Not required by /review-rusty-crate scope, but noted.)
title: Cleartext secret leaks via non-zeroized String in get/put
